summary: Gruber examines FileMaker's new personal database app Bento, responding to Daniel Jalkut's speculation that it may have originated as an iWork project before FileMaker claimed it. Gruber offers an alternative theory: Bento may have been considered for iWork but rejected because it overlaps too much with Numbers, which already handles ad-hoc database tasks like event planning and expense tracking. He argues Bento works better as a standalone product precisely because of this overlap. Gruber highlights Bento's most distinctive design choice — it's library-based rather than document-based, meaning users never deal with files, saves, or the file system. He praises this model enthusiastically and notes technical details about Bento's SQLite-backed storage and its surprisingly robust AppleScript support.
key_points: Bento visually resembles iWork apps but may have been rejected from the suite due to significant functional overlap with Numbers
Bento's library-based model, where data lives inside the app window rather than as user-managed files, fundamentally distinguishes it from document-centric iWork apps
Numbers and Bento share overlapping templates and use cases at the basic level of creating and managing lists of records
Bento stores data in an SQLite database bundle rather than a FileMaker-style database, suggesting a clean architectural break from its parent company
Bento ships with serious AppleScript support, while Numbers doesn't even have an AppleScript dictionary
money_quotes: Much of what Numbers can do is really ad-hoc database design and storage.
That they overlap at all, at such a basic level ('I'd like to create a list of records') makes it clear that Bento works better as a standalone product than as a possible fourth iWork member.
Conceptually, Bento stands apart from all three iWork apps in a fundamental way: it is not document-based.
I'm a huge fan of this library-based (rather than document-based) concept for applications.

From the user's perspective, it boils down to creating the illusion that data lives inside the application's main window rather than living as files in the file system that the user must name, locate, remember, and manage by hand.
Opening the exported file in Microsoft Excel is the primary goal of exporting CSV, as the file format is centered around the behavior application.
key_insight: Bento's library-based rather than document-based architecture represents a superior model for personal data management, freeing users from the burden of file system housekeeping.
